refashioned:
The principles examined in the preceding chapter originated in private interest and the spirit of monopoly. Those I examine in this chapter spring from national prejudice and animosity. They are, as one might expect, even more unreasonable -- and they are so even on the mercantilist system's own principles.

First, even if it were certain that free trade between France and England would produce a balance in France's favor, it would by no means follow that such trade would be disadvantageous to England, or that England's overall trade balance would be worsened. If French wines are better and cheaper than Portuguese, and French linens better and cheaper than German, it would be more advantageous for Britain to buy both its wine and its foreign linen from France rather than from Portugal and Germany. Though annual imports from France would greatly increase in value, the value of total annual imports would decrease, since the French goods of equal quality are cheaper than those of the other two countries. This would hold true even if every French good imported were consumed entirely within Britain.
elegant: The previous chapter's protectionism arose from private monopoly interests; this chapter's arises from national prejudice -- and is even more unreasonable, even by mercantilist standards. Even if free trade with France produced a bilateral deficit, it would not follow that England loses overall. If French wine and linen are cheaper than Portuguese and German equivalents, buying from France reduces total import costs -- the bilateral balance worsens but the overall balance improves.
summary: Trade restrictions based on national animosity are even more unreasonable than those based on monopoly interest. Even a bilateral trade deficit with France would not harm England's overall trade position if French goods are cheaper than alternatives.
distilled: A bilateral trade deficit with a cheaper supplier actually improves the overall trade balance by reducing total import costs.
notes: Key distinction: private interest (Chapter 4.2) vs. national prejudice (Chapter 4.3) as sources of protectionism. Smith's first argument against bilateral balance obsession: what matters is the aggregate, not any single bilateral relationship. If French goods are cheaper, buying from France reduces total spending on imports. This insight remains central to modern trade economics -- bilateral deficits are meaningless in a multilateral system. Cross-reference: 4.3.35 for Smith's full rejection of balance-of-trade doctrine.

refashioned: Second, a large part of the French goods imported might be re-exported to other countries, where, being sold at a profit, they might bring back a return equal in value to the entire original cost of the French imports. What has frequently been said of the East India trade might equally be true of French trade: that although most East India goods were bought with gold and silver, the re-exportation of some of them to other countries brought back more gold and silver than the original total cost. One of the most important branches of Dutch trade today consists in carrying French goods to other European countries. Some of the French wine drunk in Britain is actually smuggled in from Holland and Zealand. If there were either free trade between France and England, or if French goods could be imported paying only the same duties as other European nations' goods -- with those duties refunded upon re-export -- England might share in a trade that Holland finds so profitable.
elegant: Much of the French goods imported could be re-exported profitably, bringing back returns exceeding their original cost -- just as re-exported East India goods brought back more gold than was spent purchasing them. The Dutch already profit enormously from carrying French goods throughout Europe. Free Anglo-French trade would let England share in this lucrative carrying trade.
summary: Imported French goods could be re-exported at a profit, potentially returning more value than the original purchase cost. The Dutch already profit handsomely by carrying French goods to other European markets -- a trade England could share if restrictions were lifted.
distilled: Re-exportation of imported French goods could generate returns exceeding their cost, as the Dutch carrying trade already demonstrates.
notes: The re-export argument -- imports are not just for consumption but can be the basis of profitable carrying trade. The Dutch as Smith's model of profitable entrepot trade. East India trade analogy -- despite paying in gold/silver, the net metal flow was positive through re-exports. Smuggling of French wine through Holland -- restrictions create roundabout trade routes that benefit third parties. Cross-reference: 4.3.42 on direct vs. roundabout trade.

refashioned: Third and finally, there is no reliable measure by which we can determine on which side the so-called balance between any two countries actually lies, or which of them exports the greater value. National prejudice and animosity, always prompted by the private interests of particular traders, are the principles that generally guide our judgment on such questions. Two criteria have frequently been cited: the custom-house books and the course of exchange. The custom-house books, it is now generally acknowledged, are a very unreliable standard, because most goods are valued inaccurately in them. The course of exchange is perhaps equally unreliable.
elegant: There is no reliable way to determine which side of a bilateral trade balance is favored. National prejudice, driven by traders' self-interest, shapes our judgment. The two supposed measures -- customs records and exchange rates -- are both unreliable: customs books use inaccurate valuations, and exchange rates are equally misleading.
summary: There is no reliable criterion for measuring bilateral trade balances. Custom-house books use inaccurate valuations, exchange rates are equally misleading, and judgments about trade balances are typically driven by national prejudice and traders' self-interest.
distilled: Bilateral trade balances cannot be reliably measured -- both customs records and exchange rates are unreliable, leaving judgments driven by prejudice and self-interest.
notes: Third argument against bilateral balance restrictions -- the measurement problem. Custom-house books -- official trade statistics -- used notoriously inaccurate 'book of rates' valuations. Sets up the extended digression on exchange rates (4.3.7-4.3.13) and bank money (4.3.14-4.3.34). Smith's epistemological point: policy built on unmeasurable quantities is inherently unreasonable. Cross-reference: 4.3.9-4.3.13 on why computed exchange rates diverge from real exchange rates.

refashioned:
When the exchange rate between two places, such as London and Paris, is at par, it is said to indicate that debts owed from London to Paris are offset by debts owed from Paris to London. When a premium must be paid in London for a bill on Paris, it supposedly indicates that London's debts to Paris exceed Paris's debts to London, and that the difference must be sent in money -- the premium compensating for the risk, trouble, and expense of shipping it.

The ordinary state of debts and credits between two cities is said to be determined by their ordinary course of trade with each other. When neither imports more from the other than it exports, debts and credits balance. But when one imports more than it exports, it becomes indebted to the other for the excess. Debts and credits no longer offset each other, and money must be sent from the place where debts exceed credits. The ordinary course of exchange, being an indicator of the ordinary state of debt and credit between two places, is thus supposed to indicate the ordinary course of their imports and exports, since trade flows necessarily determine the state of debt and credit.
elegant: Exchange rates supposedly reflect the balance of debts between trading cities. When London-Paris exchange is at par, debts balance; a premium on Paris bills indicates London owes more, requiring money to be shipped. This state of debt and credit is said to follow from the trade balance: the city importing more becomes the debtor. Exchange rates thus supposedly indicate trade flows, since trade determines debt.
summary: Exchange rates are supposed to reflect the balance of debts between trading cities, which in turn reflects their trade balance. A premium on foreign bills indicates net indebtedness requiring monetary settlement.
distilled: Exchange rates supposedly measure trade balances by reflecting the state of debt and credit between trading partners.
notes: Begins Smith's technical critique of exchange rates as trade balance indicators. Par exchange -- when debts cancel out, no premium is needed. The premium reflects shipping costs and risks for settling in specie. Smith is setting up this conventional view in order to demolish it in subsequent paragraphs (4.3.8-4.3.13). Cross-reference: 4.3.8 on why bilateral exchange rates do not accurately reflect bilateral trade.

refashioned:
Even granting that the ordinary course of exchange reliably indicates the ordinary state of debt and credit between two places, it would not follow that the country with the favorable balance of debt and credit also has the favorable balance of trade. The state of debt and credit between two places is not entirely determined by their direct trade with each other; it is often influenced by each country's dealings with many other places.

For example, if English merchants typically pay for goods bought from Hamburg, Danzig, and Riga using bills drawn on Holland, then the state of debt and credit between England and Holland will not be determined solely by Anglo-Dutch trade, but will also be influenced by England's trade with those other places. England may be forced to send money to Holland every year even though its annual exports to Holland greatly exceed its imports from there -- even though what is called the balance of trade is very much in England's favor.
elegant: Even if exchange rates accurately reflected bilateral debts, they would not indicate bilateral trade balances. Debts between two countries are shaped by each country's trade with third parties. If English merchants pay for Baltic goods with bills on Holland, England's debt to Holland reflects Baltic trade, not just Anglo-Dutch trade. England may owe Holland money even while running a large trade surplus with it.
summary: Exchange rates between two countries reflect not just their bilateral trade but also their trade with third parties. England might owe money to Holland despite a bilateral trade surplus, simply because English merchants use Dutch bills to pay for Baltic goods.
distilled: Bilateral exchange rates are distorted by multilateral trade patterns -- a country can owe money to a trading partner with which it runs a surplus.
notes: The multilateral clearing argument -- one of Smith's most sophisticated insights. Triangular trade settlement: England pays Baltic merchants via Dutch bills, making Anglo-Dutch exchange reflect Anglo-Baltic trade. This insight anticipates modern multilateral clearing systems and undermines bilateral trade balance obsession. Hamburg, Danzig (Gdansk), Riga -- major Baltic trading cities. Cross-reference: 4.3.6 on the general unreliability of trade balance measurements.

refashioned: Moreover, the way in which par of exchange has traditionally been calculated means that the ordinary course of exchange cannot reliably indicate whether the true state of debt and credit favors the country that appears to have a favorable exchange rate. In other words, the real exchange rate may be -- and often is -- very different from the computed exchange rate, so that from the latter no reliable conclusion can be drawn about the former.
elegant: The traditional method of computing par exchange further undermines its reliability. The real exchange rate often diverges significantly from the computed rate, making the latter useless as evidence of the true state of debt and credit.
summary: Traditional methods of computing exchange rate parity are unreliable. The real exchange rate frequently diverges from the computed rate, making the latter an unreliable guide to actual trade balances.
distilled: The real exchange rate often diverges from the computed rate, making traditional par-of-exchange calculations unreliable indicators of trade balances.
notes: Transition paragraph introducing three specific reasons why computed exchange rates mislead (developed in 4.3.10-4.3.13). Distinction between 'real exchange' and 'computed exchange' -- anticipates modern concepts of nominal vs. real exchange rates. Brief but structurally important -- frames the technical arguments that follow.

refashioned: When a sum of money paid in England, containing a certain number of ounces of pure silver according to the English mint standard, purchases a bill for a sum of money in France containing an equal number of ounces of pure silver according to the French mint standard, exchange is said to be at par between the two countries. When you pay more, you are said to pay a premium, and the exchange is said to be against England and in France's favor. When you pay less, you are said to receive a premium, and the exchange is said to be against France and in England's favor.
elegant: Exchange is at par when equal weights of pure silver, measured by each country's mint standard, trade one-for-one. Paying more than par means the exchange is 'against' your country; paying less means it is 'in favor' of your country.
summary: Exchange rate parity is defined as the point where equal weights of pure silver, valued by each country's mint standard, trade evenly. Deviations from par are described as the exchange being 'against' or 'in favor of' a country.
distilled: Par exchange means equal weights of pure silver trade one-for-one between countries, with deviations described as favorable or unfavorable.
notes: Defines par of exchange in terms of pure silver content -- the metallic standard of the era. The mint standard as the theoretical benchmark vs. actual coin values (explored in 4.3.11). Establishes the vocabulary: at par, premium, against/in favor. Cross-reference: 4.3.11-4.3.12 on why this theoretical par diverges from reality.

refashioned:
First, we cannot always judge the value of a country's circulating currency by its mint standard. In some countries, coins are more worn, clipped, or otherwise degraded than in others. The actual value of any country's currency, compared with another's, depends not on the quantity of pure silver the coins should contain, but on what they actually do contain.

Before the reform of silver coinage in King William's time, the exchange between England and Holland, computed by the standard method based on their mint standards, showed twenty-five percent against England. But the value of England's circulating coins was, as Lowndes showed, more than twenty-five percent below the mint standard. The real exchange may therefore have been in England's favor even while the computed exchange was so strongly against it: a smaller number of ounces of pure silver actually paid in England may have purchased a bill for a larger number of ounces to be paid in Holland. The person supposedly paying a premium may actually have been receiving one.

Before the recent reform of English gold coinage, French coins were much less worn than English -- perhaps two or three percent closer to their standard. So if the computed exchange with France was not more than two or three percent against England, the real exchange might actually have been in England's favor. Since the gold coin reform, the exchange has been consistently in England's favor and against France.
elegant: Currency values depend not on mint standards but on the actual silver content of circulating coins, which varies with wear and clipping. Before William III's silver reform, the computed exchange showed 25% against England, but English coins were actually degraded by that same amount -- so the real exchange may have favored England. Similarly, pre-reform French coins were only 2-3% closer to standard than English, meaning a small computed disadvantage could mask a real advantage. After the gold coin reform, England's computed and real exchange both favored England.
summary: Computed exchange rates based on mint standards are misleading because circulating coins are often degraded below those standards. England's exchange appeared 25% unfavorable before silver reform, but since English coins were equally degraded, the real exchange may have actually been favorable.
distilled: Coin degradation makes computed exchange rates based on mint standards unreliable -- the real exchange can favor a country whose computed exchange appears unfavorable.
notes: First of three reasons computed exchange diverges from real exchange: coin degradation. William Lowndes -- Secretary to the Treasury, author of a 1695 report on coin degradation. The Great Recoinage of 1696 under William III -- a pivotal monetary event. The paradox: England's seemingly unfavorable exchange was actually caused by its worn coins, not by trade deficits. Cross-reference: 4.3.12 on seigniorage as a second distortion; Book I, Chapter 5 on money and value.

refashioned:
Second, in some countries the cost of minting coins is borne by the government; in others, by private individuals who bring bullion to the mint, with the government even deriving revenue from the process. In England, the government bears the coinage cost: if you bring a pound weight of standard silver to the mint, you receive back sixty-two shillings containing a pound weight of the same standard silver. In France, a duty of eight percent is deducted for coinage, which not only covers minting costs but provides a small government revenue.

In England, since coinage costs nothing to the individual, circulating coins can never be worth much more than the bullion they contain. In France, the workmanship adds value, just as it does to wrought silver plate. A sum of French money containing the same weight of pure silver as a sum of English money is therefore more valuable, requiring more bullion or goods to purchase it.

Even if both countries' coins were equally close to their mint standards, English money could not purchase an equal weight of French money, nor a bill on France for such a sum. If only enough additional money were paid to cover the French coinage cost, the real exchange might be at par even while the computed exchange showed a considerable premium in France's favor. If less were paid, the real exchange might actually favor England while the computed exchange favored France.
elegant: Seigniorage creates a second distortion. England's free coinage means coins are worth only their metal content. France's 8% coinage fee adds value -- French coins of equal silver weight are worth more because you paid for the minting. So English money cannot buy equal-weight French money at par. The computed exchange may show France favored even when the real exchange, adjusted for seigniorage, is at par or favors England.
summary: Different coinage policies distort exchange rate comparisons. England's free coinage means coins equal their bullion value; France's 8% seigniorage makes French coins of equal silver weight more valuable. This seigniorage difference can make the computed exchange appear to favor France when the real exchange is actually neutral or favors England.
distilled: Seigniorage differences between countries distort computed exchange rates -- France's 8% minting charge makes French coins of equal silver weight more valuable than English, skewing exchange calculations.
notes: Second reason for computed/real exchange divergence: seigniorage (minting charges). England's free coinage policy vs. France's 8% seigniorage. Coins with seigniorage are worth more than their metal content -- a form of value-added by government service. The wrought plate analogy -- workmanship adds value to both silverware and coins. Cross-reference: 4.3.11 on degradation; 4.3.13 on bank money as a third distortion. Anticipates modern discussions of fiat money value exceeding commodity value.

refashioned:
Third and finally, in some places -- Amsterdam, Hamburg, Venice, and others -- foreign bills of exchange are paid in what is called bank money, while in other places -- London, Lisbon, Antwerp, Leghorn -- they are paid in ordinary currency. Bank money is always worth more than the same nominal sum in common currency. A thousand guilders in the Bank of Amsterdam, for example, are worth more than a thousand guilders of Amsterdam's circulating currency. The difference is called the bank's agio, which at Amsterdam is generally about five percent.

If we suppose both countries' circulating coins are equally close to their mint standards, and one pays foreign bills in common currency while the other pays in bank money, the computed exchange may favor the bank-money country even though the real exchange favors the one paying in common currency -- for the same reason that the computed exchange may favor the country with better coins even when the real exchange does not.

Before the recent reform of English gold coins, the computed exchange was generally against London with Amsterdam, Hamburg, Venice, and other bank-money cities. But the real exchange may not have been against it at all. Since the gold coin reform, the exchange has been in London's favor even with these cities. The computed exchange has generally been in London's favor with Lisbon, Antwerp, Leghorn, and most other European places paying in common currency -- except France -- and the real exchange was probably favorable as well.
elegant: Bank money introduces a third distortion. Cities like Amsterdam and Hamburg pay foreign bills in bank money, which carries a premium (about 5% in Amsterdam) over common currency. The computed exchange therefore favors bank-money cities even when the real exchange does not. Before England's gold reform, computed exchange was against London versus Amsterdam and Hamburg, but the real exchange may have been neutral or favorable. Since the reform, both computed and real exchange favor London.
summary: Bank money, worth more than common currency by a premium called the agio, creates a third distortion in exchange rate comparisons. Cities paying in bank money appear favored in computed exchange even when the real balance is neutral or against them.
distilled: The premium on bank money over common currency creates a third systematic distortion between computed and real exchange rates.
notes: Third reason for computed/real exchange divergence: bank money vs. common currency. The agio -- the premium of bank money over circulating currency (about 5% in Amsterdam). Bank-money cities: Amsterdam, Hamburg, Venice. Common-currency cities: London, Lisbon, Antwerp. Sets up the digression on deposit banks (4.3.14-4.3.34). England's gold coin reform as a watershed -- before and after comparisons throughout. Cross-reference: 4.3.14-4.3.34 for the extended Bank of Amsterdam digression.

refashioned:
Digression concerning Banks of Deposit, particularly concerning that of Amsterdam.

The currency of a great state like France or England generally consists almost entirely of its own coins. If this currency becomes worn, clipped, or otherwise degraded below its standard value, the state can effectively restore it by reforming its coinage. But the currency of a small state like Genoa or Hamburg can rarely consist entirely of its own coins. It must largely be composed of coins from all the neighboring states with which its inhabitants constantly trade. Such a state therefore cannot always fix its currency simply by reforming its own coins. If foreign bills of exchange are paid in this mixed currency, the uncertain value of any sum denominated in such inherently uncertain money must make the exchange rate consistently unfavorable to the small state, whose currency is inevitably valued abroad at even less than it is actually worth.
elegant: Large states can fix degraded currency by reforming their own coinage. Small trading states cannot -- their currency is a mix of foreign coins beyond their control. Paying foreign bills in such uncertain currency makes the exchange consistently unfavorable, with the currency valued abroad at even less than its actual worth.
summary: Large states can restore degraded currency through coinage reform, but small trading states cannot because their currency consists largely of foreign coins. This makes their exchange rates permanently unfavorable, as markets discount uncertain currency below its actual value.
distilled: Small trading states with mixed foreign-coin currencies face permanently unfavorable exchange rates because they cannot control the quality of their circulating money.
notes: Opens the Bank of Amsterdam digression (4.3.14-4.3.34). The small-state monetary problem: currency quality beyond sovereign control. Genoa and Hamburg as examples of small trading states. This monetary vulnerability motivated the creation of deposit banks -- providing a reliable unit of account. Cross-reference: 4.3.15 on how deposit banks solve this problem; 4.3.17 on the founding of the Bank of Amsterdam.

refashioned:
To remedy the disadvantage this unfavorable exchange imposed on their merchants, such small states, when they began paying attention to trade interests, frequently enacted that foreign bills of exchange above a certain value should be paid not in common currency but by an order on, or a transfer in the books of, a bank established under the credit and protection of the state. This bank was always obliged to pay in good, true money exactly matching the state's standard.

The banks of Venice, Genoa, Amsterdam, Hamburg, and Nuremberg all seem to have been originally established for this purpose, though some were later made to serve other ends as well. Because bank money was better than common currency, it naturally carried a premium -- an agio that was larger or smaller depending on how far the common currency had degraded below the state's standard. The agio of the Bank of Hamburg, for example, commonly about fourteen percent, represents the supposed difference between the state's good standard money and the clipped, worn, and diminished currency flowing in from all neighboring states.
elegant: Small trading states solved their currency problem by requiring large foreign bills to be paid through deposit banks guaranteed by the state. These banks paid in good standard money, naturally commanding a premium (agio) over degraded common currency. The Bank of Hamburg's 14% agio reflected the gap between the state's monetary standard and the worn foreign coins circulating as common currency.
summary: Small trading states established deposit banks, backed by the state, to provide reliable money for settling foreign bills. Bank money naturally carried a premium over degraded common currency -- about 14% at Hamburg, reflecting the difference between standard and actual coin quality.
distilled: Deposit banks were created by small trading states to provide reliable standard money for foreign exchange, naturally commanding a premium over degraded circulating currency.
notes: The deposit bank solution -- a state-guaranteed monetary standard alongside degraded circulating currency. Five major deposit banks: Venice, Genoa, Amsterdam, Hamburg, Nuremberg. The agio as a market-determined quality premium -- 14% at Hamburg reflects severe currency degradation. 'Originally established with this view, though some afterwards made subservient to other purposes' -- foreshadows the Bank of Amsterdam's evolution. Cross-reference: 4.3.16-4.3.17 on Amsterdam's specific founding circumstances.

refashioned: Before 1609, the enormous quantity of clipped and worn foreign coins that Amsterdam's extensive trade brought in from all over Europe had reduced the value of its circulating currency to about nine percent below that of freshly minted good money. Good money, no sooner appearing, was melted down or carried away, as it always is in such circumstances. Merchants, despite having plenty of currency, could not always find enough good money to pay their bills of exchange. The value of those bills, despite several regulations attempting to prevent it, became largely uncertain.
elegant: Before 1609, Amsterdam's extensive trade flooded it with degraded foreign coins, reducing its currency to 9% below mint value. Good coins were immediately melted down or exported. Merchants had plenty of currency but could not find enough good money for bills of exchange, whose value became dangerously uncertain.
summary: Before 1609, Amsterdam's currency had degraded to 9% below mint value due to an influx of worn foreign coins. Good money was immediately melted or exported, and merchants could not reliably settle bills of exchange.
distilled: Amsterdam's pre-1609 currency crisis -- 9% degradation, good money driven out, unreliable bill settlement -- necessitated the founding of its deposit bank.
notes: The founding problem that created the Bank of Amsterdam. Gresham's Law in action: bad money drives out good (good coins 'melted down or carried away'). The 9% degradation figure -- precise enough to quantify the problem. Currency uncertainty as a commercial crisis -- merchants cannot reliably settle international obligations. Cross-reference: 4.3.17 on the bank's founding in 1609; Book I, Chapter 5 on Gresham's Law dynamics.

refashioned:
To remedy these problems, a bank was established in 1609 under the guarantee of the city. This bank received both foreign coins and the country's own light and worn coins at their real intrinsic value in good standard money, deducting only enough to cover minting costs and other necessary management expenses. For the remaining value, it gave a credit in its books. This credit was called bank money, which, since it represented money exactly according to the mint standard, was always of the same real value and was intrinsically worth more than circulating currency.

At the same time, it was enacted that all bills drawn on or negotiated at Amsterdam of 600 guilders or more must be paid in bank money. This immediately eliminated all uncertainty in the value of such bills. Every merchant, as a consequence of this regulation, was obliged to maintain an account with the bank in order to pay his foreign bills of exchange, which necessarily created a steady demand for bank money.
elegant: The Bank of Amsterdam, founded in 1609 under the city's guarantee, accepted all coins at their true intrinsic value (minus minting costs) and issued book credits -- bank money -- representing exact mint-standard value. A law requiring all bills of 600 guilders or more to be settled in bank money eliminated exchange uncertainty and forced every merchant to maintain a bank account, creating constant demand for this superior money.
summary: The Bank of Amsterdam was founded in 1609 to receive coins at intrinsic value and issue standardized book credits. A legal requirement that large bills be settled in bank money eliminated exchange uncertainty and created steady demand for bank accounts.
distilled: The Bank of Amsterdam solved currency uncertainty by issuing standardized book credits and mandating their use for large transactions, creating a reliable monetary standard.
notes: Founding of the Bank of Amsterdam in 1609 -- one of the most important monetary innovations in European history. Key design features: coins accepted at intrinsic (not face) value; credits denominated in mint-standard money; legal requirement for large transactions. The 600-guilder threshold -- creates a two-tier monetary system (bank money for wholesale, currency for retail). Mandatory use creates built-in demand -- a crucial design insight. Cross-reference: 4.3.18 on the additional advantages of bank money.

refashioned:
Bank money, beyond its intrinsic superiority over common currency and the additional value this mandatory demand gave it, had several other advantages. It was safe from fire, robbery, and other accidents. The city of Amsterdam guaranteed it. It could be transferred by a simple book entry, without the trouble of counting coins or the risk of physically transporting money from place to place.

Because of these various advantages, bank money seems to have commanded a premium from the very beginning. It is generally believed that all the money originally deposited in the bank was allowed to remain there, since no one wanted to withdraw a deposit that could be sold at a premium in the market. By demanding payment from the bank, the owner of a bank credit would lose this premium. Just as a freshly minted shilling buys no more in the market than a common worn shilling, so the good money withdrawn from the bank's vaults, once mixed with ordinary circulation, would be worth no more than common currency and could no longer be easily distinguished from it.

While it remained in the bank's vaults, its superior quality was known and certified. Once in private hands, verifying that superiority would require more effort than the difference was worth. Withdrawal would also sacrifice all of bank money's other advantages -- its security, easy transferability, and usefulness for paying foreign bills. On top of all this, withdrawal required paying a storage fee.
elegant: Bank money offered additional advantages beyond its intrinsic superiority: security from fire and theft, city guarantee, and effortless transfer by book entry. These benefits created a permanent premium. No one withdrew deposits because good money, once mixed with common circulation, lost its certified quality and became indistinguishable from worn coins. Withdrawing meant sacrificing the premium, the security, the transferability, and paying a storage fee besides.
summary: Bank money commanded a permanent premium because it offered security, government guarantee, and easy transfer. No one withdrew deposits since good money mixed into circulation would lose its certified quality and become indistinguishable from common worn coins.
distilled: Bank money's security, guarantee, transferability, and certified quality created a permanent premium that made withdrawal irrational.
notes: Why bank money commanded a premium even beyond its metallic superiority -- convenience, security, certification. The 'mixing problem': certified good money loses its certification once withdrawn into general circulation. Gresham's Law in reverse: good money stays in the bank because it is more valuable there. The premium as an equilibrium: no rational actor withdraws because withdrawal destroys value. Cross-reference: 4.3.19 on bullion deposits; modern parallels to insured bank deposits.

refashioned:
The original deposits of coin -- those which the bank was bound to return in coin -- constituted the bank's original capital, representing the entire value behind what was called bank money. These coin deposits are now believed to constitute only a very small part of the total.

To facilitate the bullion trade, the bank has for many years been giving credit in its books for deposits of gold and silver bullion, generally at about five percent below the mint price. At the same time, it issues what is called a receipt, entitling the depositor or bearer to withdraw the bullion within six months, provided they transfer to the bank a quantity of bank money equal to the credit originally given, and pay a storage fee of one-quarter percent for silver or one-half percent for gold. If this payment is not made and the six months expire, the deposit becomes the bank's property at the price at which it was originally received.

What is paid for storing the deposit may be considered a kind of warehouse rent. Several reasons have been offered for why this rent is higher for gold than for silver. Gold's fineness, it is said, is harder to verify. Fraud is easier and more costly with the more precious metal. Silver, being the standard metal, is one the state supposedly wishes to encourage depositing more than gold.
elegant: The bank's original capital was coin deposits it was bound to return. To facilitate the bullion trade, it began issuing credits for bullion deposits at 5% below mint price, with receipts allowing withdrawal within six months upon returning the equivalent bank money plus a storage fee (0.25% for silver, 0.5% for gold). Unclaimed deposits become the bank's property. The higher gold fee reflects the greater difficulty of verifying gold's fineness and the state's preference for encouraging silver deposits.
summary: The Bank of Amsterdam expanded beyond coin deposits to accept bullion at 5% below mint price, issuing receipts redeemable within six months upon payment of storage fees. Unclaimed deposits revert to the bank. This bullion deposit system facilitated Amsterdam's role as Europe's bullion trading center.
distilled: The Bank of Amsterdam evolved from a coin deposit bank into a bullion warehouse, issuing credits at 5% below mint price with time-limited, fee-bearing receipts.
notes: Evolution of the Bank of Amsterdam from coin depository to bullion warehouse. The receipt system -- a derivative instrument: separate from bank credit, tradeable, time-limited. 5% discount below mint price -- the bank's margin for providing the service. Differential warehouse rents: 0.25% silver vs. 0.5% gold -- reflects verification costs and policy preference. 'Falls to the bank' -- unclaimed deposits as bank revenue (explored further in 4.3.26). Cross-reference: 4.3.23-4.3.25 on how receipts and bank credits interact in the market.
